While the symptoms would meet red flag criteria for adults, CRR X-ray pathways specifically exclude children under 16 years.

Knee examination is normal, no fever, mild limp, restricted ROM R hip with mild pain. No recent illness. Clinical assessment suggests possible hip pathology as cause of referred knee pain. Red flags assessed: [document presence/absence of persistent deep pain unrelated to activity, unexplained night pain, bony swelling, joint deformity].
ai_response.redirect: Adolescent with knee pain and restricted hip ROM - consider assessment for slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE) or other hip pathology causing referred knee pain

results.previous_verdict_v1: Proceeds — referred knee pain with restricted hip ROM — SUFE concern

results.notes: v2.0.0 declined — model redirected to SUFE assessment rather than accepting. Hip X-ray criteria require specific clinical features not documented; model says no OA red flags but misses the knee-pain-with-restricted-hip-ROM SUFE pathway. Possible criteria data gap or model error on paediatric hip.

ai_response.notes: Microscopic haematuria in female patients of any age is not routinely funded under CRR criteria. The criteria specifically require either: (P3) male patient older than 50 years who is current or ex-smoker, or (P4) male patient younger than 50 years. Female patients with microscopic haematuria do not meet funding criteria regardless of age or smoking status.
